summary: Gruber corrects Daniel Eran Dilger's claim that Android requires task killer apps, arguing that Android's multitasking model is actually quite similar to what Apple unveiled in iPhone OS 4. He explains that both platforms use a new paradigm where the system, not the user, manages app lifecycles — apps can be killed at any moment to free resources. The piece contrasts this new mobile model with traditional desktop operating systems where processes persist until the user quits them and swap space masks memory limits. Gruber argues the real story isn't iPhone vs. Android multitasking differences (which are minor), but the fundamental shift from process-priority to user-responsiveness-priority in mobile computing.
key_points: Android and iPhone OS 4 multitasking are broadly similar — both automatically manage background apps and kill least-recently-used processes when memory runs low
The fundamental shift in mobile computing is from process persistence (desktop model) to system responsiveness (mobile model), where apps must be ready to quit at any moment
Mobile devices lack swap space, so instead of slowing to a crawl like desktops, they simply kill background apps to stay responsive
The Android vs. iPhone multitasking debate is about fine details, not the big picture — Android offers more developer freedom while iPhone prioritizes controlled responsiveness
Apple's use of blocks and Grand Central Dispatch in iPhone OS 4 represents state-of-the-art computer science, hinting at future multi-core mobile processors
money_quotes: If you want to understand the current mobile landscape, it's essential to understand that, in broad terms, what Apple has unveiled for iPhone OS 4 is pretty similar to Android's multitasking model.
Whatever you think of iPhone OS 4's multitasking model, don't make the mistake of thinking it's simple; this is state of the art computer science.
In the old model, processes that have already been launched get priority — once running, they stay running. In the new model, the user's intentions get priority.
On iPhone and Android, apps don't decide when to quit. They must be ready to quit on short notice at any time.
In the traditional model, the primary priority is keeping everything open. In this new model, the primary priority is that the system must always be responsive to the user.
You know what iPhone OS is missing that Mac OS X has? The SPOD.
No matter how much RAM future devices contain, it's never again going to be acceptable to make users wait before launching or switching contexts.
key_insight: The real revolution in mobile multitasking isn't the differences between iPhone and Android, but the shared paradigm shift from prioritizing process persistence to prioritizing instant user responsiveness.
